elements.sent-2411-001: We apply the well known Rayleigh-Ritz method (RRM) to the projection of a Hamiltonian operator chosen recently for the extension of the variational principle to ensemble states.
elements.sent-2411-002: By means of a toy model we show that the RRM eigenvalues approach to those of the projected Hamiltonian from below.
elements.sent-2411-003: The Rayleigh-Ritz method (RRM) is one of the most widely used approaches for the study of the electronic structure of atoms and molecules\cite{P68,SO96 .}
elements.sent-2411-004: One of its main advantages is that the RRM eigenvalues converge from above towards the exact energies of the physical system\cite{M33 (see also\cite {F22 and references therein).}}
elements.sent-2411-005: In their introduction to the Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle Ding et al \cite{DHS24 resorted to a most curious Hamiltonian operator on a $D$ -dimensional Hilbert space.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Although such an operator is quite unrealistic for the treatment of actual physical problems, it seems to be worth further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-007: The RRM applies to any Hermitian operator $H$ with eigenvalues $E_{k}$ and eigenvectors $\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $ \begin{equation}H\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle =E_{k}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle ,\;k=0,1,\ldots . \label{eq:eigen_eq}\end{equation} If we have a complete set of non-orthogonal vectors $\left| u_{i}\right\rangle $, $i=0,1,\ldots $, then the approximate RRM eigenvalues $ W_{n}$ are roots of the secular determinant\cite{P68,SO96,F24 \begin{equation}\left| \mathbf{H}-W\mathbf{S}\right| =0, \label{eq:sec_det}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{S}$ are $N\times N$ matrices with elements $ H_{ij =\left\langle u_{i}\right| H\left| u_{j}\right\rangle $ and $ S_{ij =\left\langle u_{i}\right.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: It is well known that $W_{k}\geq E_{k}$ for all $k=0,1,\ldots ,N-1$\cite{P68,SO96,M33,F22,F24 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In their introduction to the Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle, Ding et al \cite{DHS24 considered the projection \begin{equation}H_{D}=\sum_{k=0}^{D-1}E_{k}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| , \label{eq:H_D}\end{equation} of $H$ on a subspace $\mathcal{H _{D}$ of dimension $D$ spanned by the set of eigenvectors $\mathcal{S _{D =\left\{ \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle ,\;k=0,1,\ldots ,D-1\right\} $.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: They correctly stated that \begin{equation}\frac{\left\langle \psi \right| H_{D}\left| \psi \right\rangle }{ \left\langle \psi \right. \left| \psi \right\rangle }\geq E_{0}\;\forall \psi \in \mathcal{H}_{D}. \label{eq:var_princ_D}\end{equation} This introduction of the variational principle is rather unrealistic and of scarce utility because in practical applications we do not know $\mathcal{H _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: If we already know $\mathcal{H _{D}$ then we can choose a complete basis set $\mathcal{B =\left\{ \left| i\right\rangle ,\;i=0,1,\ldots ,D-1\right\} $ of orthonormal vectors and, consequently, the diagonalization of the matrix of $H_{D}$ in such a basis is a trivial problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For this reason, in what follows we assume that $\mathcal{H _{D}$ is unknown.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Typically, we have to deal with a Hamiltonian operator $H$ defined on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ and we do not know its eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, we can try and do something interesting with the unrealistic scheme of Ding et al by simply assuming that $\mathcal{H}\subset \mathcal{H _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: If we assume that $H_{D}$ is also defined on $\mathcal{H}$ we can apply the RRM to this projected Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-016: In such a case, the matrix elements are given by \begin{equation}\left( H_{D}\right) _{ij}=\sum_{k=0}^{D-1}E_{k}\left\langle u_{i}\right. \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right. \left| u_{j}\right\rangle , \label{eq:(H_D)_ij}\end{equation} where $\left\{ \left| u_{j}\right\rangle ,\;j=0,1,\ldots \right\} $ spans the Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-017: We do not assume the vectors $\left| u_{j}\right\rangle $ to be orthonormal.
elements.sent-2411-018: Under the conditions given above the results may be unexpected.
elements.sent-2411-020: We appreciate that it is possible to obtain lower bounds instead of upper ones.
elements.sent-2411-021: A suitable toy model is given by \begin{equation}H=-\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}, \label{eq:H_model}\end{equation} with the boundary conditions $\psi (0)=\psi (1)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-022: This example was chosen in recent discussions of the RRM\cite{F22,F24 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The exact eigenvalues and eigenfunctions are \begin{equation}E_{k}=\frac{k^{2}\pi ^{2}}{2},\;\psi _{k}(x)=\sqrt{2}\sin \left( k\pi x\right) ,\;k=1,2,\ldots . \label{eq:E_k,psi_k_example}\end{equation} For simplicity, we choose the non-orthogonal basis set \begin{equation}u_{i}(x)=x^{i}(1-x),\;i=1,2,\ldots , \label{eq:u_i(x)}\end{equation} already used earlier\cite{F24 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Table~\ref{tab:RRH shows that the RRM eigenvalues $W_{k}$ converge from above towards the exact eigenvalues $E_{k}$ as expected\cite{P68,SO96,M33,F22,F24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Tables \ref{tab:D=1 , \ref{tab:D=2 and \ref{tab:D=3 show the RRM results for the projected Hamiltonian $H_{D}$ with $D=1$, $D=2$ and $D=3$, respectively (note that $k=1,2,\ldots ,D$ in this example).}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We appreciate that there are $D$ meaningful eigenvalues $W_{k}\neq 0$, $k=1,2,\ldots ,D$, and the remaining roots vanish $W_{k =0$, $D<k\leq N$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: A most curious fact is that the RRM eigenvalues $W_{k}$ converge towards the exact ones $E_{k}$ from below.
elements.sent-2411-028: The well known proofs for the upper bounds mentioned above \cite {M33,F22 (and references therein) do not apply here because the functions $ u_{i (x)$ cannot be expressed in terms of the finite set $\left\{ \psi _{k (x),\;k=1,2,\ldots ,D\right\} $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: For every RRM eigenvalue $W_{k}$ we obtain an approximate solution\cite {P68,SO96,F24 \begin{equation}\left| \varphi _{k}\right\rangle =\sum_{j=1}^{N}c_{jk}\left| u_{j}\right\rangle , \label{eq:varphi_k}\end{equation} and we choose such solutions to be orthonormal $\left\langle \varphi _{k}\right.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The results of tables \ref{tab:D=1 , \ref{tab:D=2 and \ref{tab:D=3 suggest that \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| H_{D}\left| \varphi _{j}\right\rangle \left| \varphi _{i}\right\rangle \left\langle \varphi _{j}\right| =\sum_{i=1}^{D}W_{i}\left| \varphi _{i}\right\rangle \left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| , \label{eq:spectral_H_D}\end{equation} because $\left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| H_{D}\left| \varphi _{j}\right\rangle =W_{i}\delta _{ij}$\cite{P68,SO96,F24 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: We can extend the results of the preceding subsection to a more general case based on the same toy model.
elements.sent-2411-032: Suppose that we have a set of orthonormal vectors $\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $, $k=$ $1,2,\ldots ,D$ and construct the projected Hamiltonian operator \begin{equation}H_{D}=\sum_{k=1}^{D}\alpha _{k}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| , \label{eq:H_D_general}\end{equation} where $\alpha _{k}$ are arbitrary real numbers.
elements.sent-2411-033: We can obviously apply the RRM as in the preceding example.
elements.sent-2411-034: Table~\ref{tab:H_D_gen shows RRM eigenvalues for the case $D=3$ and $\alpha _{k =k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is not clear to us why Ding et al\cite{DHS24 introduced the variational principle by means of an unrealistic projected Hamiltonian because this principle as well as the properties of the RRM have already been proved for the kind of operators commonly found in actual physical problems\cite {M33,F22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, when the projected Hamiltonian is treated by the RRM in the usual way, one obtains some curious results as shown above.
elements.sent-2411-037: One of them is the occurrence of null eigenvalues and the other is the appearance of lower bounds instead of upper ones (compare table~\ref{tab:RRH with tables \ref{tab:D=1 , \ref{tab:D=2 and \ref{tab:D=3 ).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-038: By means of a toy model we show that the RRM eigenvalues approach to those of the projected Hamiltonian from below in most cases but a few ones .
elements.sent-2411-039: They correctly stated that \begin{equation}\frac{\left\langle \psi \right| H_{D}\left| \psi \right\rangle }{ \left\langle \psi \right. \left| \psi \right\rangle }\geq E_{0}\;\forall \psi \in \mathcal{H}_{D}. \label{eq:var_princ_D}\end{equation} This introduction of the variational principle is far from what we commonly face in ordinary applications of nonrelativistic quantum mechanics where we do not know $\mathcal{H _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, we can try and do something interesting with the projected Hamiltonian of Ding et al by simply assuming that $\mathcal{H}\subset \mathcal{H _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Tables \ref{tab:D=1 and \ref{tab:D=2 show the RRM results for the projected Hamiltonian $H_{D}$ with $D=1$ and $D=2$, respectively (note that $ k=1,2,\ldots ,D$ in this example).}}
elements.sent-2411-042: It is interesting that the RRM yields the eigenvalues $E=0$ exactly while the others are approximate (though, they converge towards the exact ones as $N$ increases).
elements.sent-2411-043: However, this result is not general.
elements.sent-2411-044: In table~\ref{tab:D=3 we show RRM eigenvalues for $D=3$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We see that this approach yields an upper bound to $E_{1}$ for $N=1$ and lower bounds to all the eigenvalues for $N>1$ .
elements.sent-2411-046: The well known proofs for the upper bounds mentioned above \cite{M33,F22 (and references therein) do not apply here because the functions $u_{i (x)$ cannot be expressed in terms of the finite set $\left\{ \psi _{k (x),\;k=1,2,\ldots ,D\right\} $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In order to investigate the particular case $N=1$ in more detail we calculated the expectation value $\left\langle H_{D}\right\rangle $ with the function $u_{1 (x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The results in table~\ref{tab:N=1 clearly show that $ \left\langle H_{1}\right\rangle <E_{1 <\left\langle H\right\rangle =5$ and $ E_{1 <\left\langle H_{D}\right\rangle <\left\langle H\right\rangle $ for $ D>1 $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The table suggests the obvious conclusion that $\lim\limits_{D \rightarrow \infty }\left\langle H_{D}\right\rangle =\left\langle H\right\rangle $ that can be proved analytically: \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty }\frac{k^{2}\pi ^{2}}{2}\frac{\left\langle u_{1}\right. \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right. \left| u_{1}\right\rangle }{\left\langle u_{1}\right. \left| u_{1}\right\rangle }= \frac{240}{\pi ^{4}}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty }\frac{1-(-1)^{k}}{k^{4}}=5.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: It is worth adding that for $D=4$ we have $W_{1 >E_{1}$ for $N=1$ as aready discussed and also $W_{2 >E_{2}$ for $N=3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We also carried out some numerical experiments with $E_{k =k^{2}\pi ^{2 /2+c$ , where $c$ is a real constant.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Table~\ref{tab:D=3_shifted shows that for $ c=-5$ $W_{1}$ becomes an upper bound while $W_{2}$ and $W_{3}$ remain lower bounds.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In this case we appreciate that $W_{1 >1$ for $N=1$ and $W_{1 <1$ for all $N>1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Also $ W_{2 >2$ for $N=3$ and $W_{2 <2$ for all $N>3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: We conclude that the RRM yields upper and lower bounds for this kind of projected Hamiltonian operators.
elements.sent-2411-056: It is not clear to us why Ding et al\cite{DHS24 introduced the variational principle by means of a projected Hamiltonian operator because this principle as well as the properties of the RRM have already been proved for the kind of operators commonly found in actual physical problems\cite {M33,F22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, when the RRM is applied to the projected Hamiltonian in the usual way, one obtains lower bounds in most cases as shown above.
elements.sent-2411-058: It is interesting that the null $N-D$ eigenvalues are given exactly for all $N>D$ although the linear combinations of the approximate basis vectors $\left| u_{i}\right\rangle $ only yield the exact ones $\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $ in the limit $N\rightarrow \infty $.

elements.sent-2411-059: By means of a toy model we show that the RRM eigenvalues approach to those of the projected Hamiltonian from below in most cases but a few ones.
elements.sent-2411-060: We also discuss the effect of an energy shift and the projection of the identity operator.
elements.sent-2411-061: In the calculations discussed above we have considered the identity operator on $\mathcal{H}$ \begin{equation}I=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty }\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| , \label{eq:I}\end{equation} so that the overlap matrix is given by $S_{ij =\left\langle u_{i}\right| I\left| u_{j}\right\rangle $, $i,j=1,2,\ldots ,N$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: We obtain completely different results if we consider the projection of $I$ on $\mathcal{H _{D}$ \begin{equation}I_{D}=\sum_{k=1}^{D}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| , \label{eq:I_D}\end{equation} and the overlap matrix $S_{i,j ^{D =\left\langle u_{i}\right| I_{D}\left| u_{j}\right\rangle $, $i,j=1,2,\ldots ,N$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In this case, we obtain upper bounds for $1\leq N<D$ and the exact eigenvalues when $N=D$.
elements.sent-2411-064: For example, for $D=N=5$ we have \begin{equation}\left| \mathbf{H}_{D}-W\mathbf{S}^{D}\right| =\frac{67108864\left( \pi ^{2}-2W\right) \left( 2\pi ^{2}-W\right) \left( 8\pi ^{2}-W\right) \left( 9\pi ^{2}-2W\right) \left( 25\pi ^{2}-2W\right) }{9765625\pi ^{46}}, \label{eq:sec_det_N=D=5}\end{equation} that yields the fifth lowest eigenvalues exactly.
elements.sent-2411-065: We have also shown that the RRM yields upper bounds for $1\leq N<D$ and exact eigenvalues for $ N=D$ when we consider the identity operator (\ref{eq:I_D ) instead of (\ref {eq:I ).}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We apply the well known Rayleigh-Ritz method (RRM) to the projection of a Hamiltonian operator chosen recently for the extension of the Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle to ensemble states.
elements.sent-2411-067: In a recent paper, Ding et al\cite{DHS24 proposed an extension of the Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle to ensemble states.}
elements.sent-2411-068: In their introduction to the principle for pure states they resorted to a most curious Hamiltonian operator on a $D$-dimensional Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-069: In section~\ref {sec:unrealistic we outline the formulation of the Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle as given by Ding et al. In section~\ref{sec:RRM we discuss the application of the RRM to the projected Hamiltonian introduced by those authors.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Finally, in section~\ref{sec:conclusions se summarize the main results and draw conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In order to avoid misconceptions we resort to the exact expression of Ding et al: ``As far as the computation of ground states is concerned, the effectiveness of numerous numerical methods rests on the underlying Rayleigh-Ritz (RR) variational principle: For any Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\hat{H}=\sum_{k=0}^{D-1}E_{k}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| \label{eq:H_DHS}\end{equation} on a $D$-dimensional Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ the ground state energy $ E_{0}$ can be obtained by variational means according to \begin{equation}E_{0}\leq \left\langle \psi \right| \hat{H}\left| \psi \right\rangle ,\;\forall \left| \psi \right\rangle \in \mathcal{H}. \label{eq:Variational_DHS}\end{equation} The extremality within the energy spectrum of the sought-after minimal eigenvalue $E_{0}$ of $\hat{H}$ is absolutely crucial for the effectiveness and predictive power of variational ground state approaches.
elements.sent-2411-072: It namely implies that the accuracy of a trial state $\left| \psi \right\rangle $ increases as the variational energy $\left\langle \psi \right| \hat{H}\left| \psi \right\rangle $ approaches the ground state energy $E_{0}$, and equality with the exact ground state is reached if and only if the variational energy equals $E_{0}$''.
elements.sent-2411-073: From the formulation of the problem we understand that $E_{k}$ and $\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $ are eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $\hat{H}$ and that we know the $D$-dimensional Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ because we are able to pick up a trial state $\left| \psi \right\rangle $ belonging to it.
elements.sent-2411-074: In such a case we can also choose a complete orthonormal basis set $\mathcal{ B =\left\{ \left| i\right\rangle ,\;i=0,1,\ldots ,D-1\right\} $ for $ \mathcal{H}$ and build the $D\times D$ matrix representation $\mathbf{H}$ of $\hat{H}$ with matrix elements $H_{ij =\left\langle i\right| \hat{H}\left| j\right\rangle $.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Consequently, the diagonalization of $\mathbf{H}$ gives us the exact eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $\hat{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, in practice we unfortunately never face such an advantageous situation.
elements.sent-2411-077: The RRM was developed to deal with actual problems in which the Hilbert space is not finite-dimensional\cite{M33,F22 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: In such cases the RRM yields approximate eigenvalues and eigenvectors and their accuracy increases with the dimension $N$ of the matrix representation of the Hamiltonian operator.
elements.sent-2411-079: The exact result is expected to be obtained in the limit $N\rightarrow \infty $.
elements.sent-2411-080: For this reason, we have called the model introduced by Ding et al unrealistic.
elements.sent-2411-081: It seems to be interesting to apply the RRM to the unrealistic problem outlined in section~ \ref{sec:unrealistic .}
elements.sent-2411-082: In this subsection we modify the notation of section~\ref{sec:unrealistic in order to facilitate the discussion.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We assume that we have a Hamiltonian operator $H$ defined on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ an focus our attention on the projection \begin{equation}H_{D}=\sum_{k=0}^{D-1}E_{k}\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right| , \label{eq:H_D}\end{equation} of $H$ on a subspace $\mathcal{H _{D}$ of dimension $D$ spanned by the set of eigenvectors $\mathcal{S _{D =\left\{ \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle ,\;k=0,1,\ldots ,D-1\right\} $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In order to apply the RRM to $H_{D}$ we need the matrix elements \begin{equation}\left( H_{D}\right) _{ij}=\sum_{k=0}^{D-1}E_{k}\left\langle u_{i}\right. \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right. \left| u_{j}\right\rangle , \label{eq:(H_D)_ij}\end{equation} where $\left\{ \left| u_{j}\right\rangle ,\;j=0,1,\ldots \right\} $ spans the Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Under the conditions given above the results may be somewhat unexpected.
elements.sent-2411-088: We appreciate that there are $D$ eigenvalues $W_{k}\neq 0$, $k=1,2,\ldots ,D$, and the remaining roots vanish $W_{k =0$, $D<k\leq N$ as expected.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We appreciate that in these particular cases the RRM eigenvalues $W_{k}$ converge towards the exact ones $E_{k}$ from below.
elements.sent-2411-090: In table~ \ref{tab:D=3 we show RRM eigenvalues for $D=3$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We see that this approach yields an upper bound to $E_{1}$ for $N=1$ and lower bounds to all the eigenvalues for $N>1$.
elements.sent-2411-092: For every RRM eigenvalue $W_{k}$ we obtain an approximate solution\cite {P68,SO96,F24 \begin{equation}\left| \varphi _{k}\right\rangle =\sum_{j=1}^{N}c_{jk}\left| u_{j}\right\rangle , \label{eq:varphi_k}\end{equation} and we commonly choose such solutions to be orthonormal $\left\langle \varphi _{k}\right.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The table suggests the obvious conclusion that $\lim\limits_{D \rightarrow \infty }\left\langle H_{D}\right\rangle =\left\langle H\right\rangle $ which can be proved analytically: \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty }\frac{k^{2}\pi ^{2}}{2}\frac{\left\langle u_{1}\right. \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle \left\langle \psi _{k}\right. \left| u_{1}\right\rangle }{\left\langle u_{1}\right. \left| u_{1}\right\rangle }= \frac{240}{\pi ^{4}}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty }\frac{1-(-1)^{k}}{k^{4}}=5.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: It is worth adding that for $D=4$ we have $W_{1 >E_{1}$ for $N=1$, as already pointed out for $D=3$, and also $W_{2 >E_{2}$ for $N=3$.}}

elements.sent-2411-108: From the results of tables \ref{tab:D=1 , \ref{tab:D=2 and \ref{tab:D=3 and $ \left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| H_{D}\left| \varphi _{j}\right\rangle =W_{i}\delta _{ij}$\cite{P68,SO96,F24 we conclude that \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| H_{D}\left| \varphi _{j}\right\rangle \left| \varphi _{i}\right\rangle \left\langle \varphi _{j}\right| =\sum_{i=1}^{D}W_{i}\left| \varphi _{i}\right\rangle \left\langle \varphi _{i}\right| . \label{eq:spectral_H_D}\end{equation} In order to investigate the particular case $N=1$ in more detail we calculated the expectation value $\left\langle H_{D}\right\rangle $ with the function $u_{1 (x)$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-110: It is interesting that the null $ N-D$ eigenvalues are given exactly for all $N>D$ although the approximate RRM solutions $\left| \varphi _{j}\right\rangle $ only yield the exact ones $ \left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $ in the limit $N\rightarrow \infty $.
elements.sent-2411-111: As argued above, it is only necessary that the approximate RRM solutions are orthogonal to the exact ones $\left| \psi _{k}\right\rangle $ in $\mathcal{H _{D}$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: We have also shown that the RRM yields upper bounds for $1\leq N<D$ and exact eigenvalues for $N=D$ when we consider the identity operator (\ref {eq:I_D ) instead of (\ref{eq:I ).}}
